ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 226355009 refers to cooked rice broadly; no white-rice-specific code identified. Used as closest parent concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of steamed white rice as served in a Singaporean hawker centre or coffee shop, approximately 180 g cooked weight.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked White Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010185 is sourced from HPB Singapore with provenance 'canonical' and confidence 0.95. The per-100g values are accepted as reported. Note: the reported values (protein 6.5 g, fat 4.21 g, dietary fibre 3.7 g, sodium 231 mg) are atypically high for plain steamed white rice and may reflect a composite dish or enriched preparation. These values are preserved as-is from the HPB canonical record. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168878, 'Rice, white, long-grain, regular, cooked') reports energy 130 kcal, protein 2.69 g, fat 0.28 g, carbohydrate 28.17 g, fibre 0.4 g, sodium 1 mg per 100 g — substantially different. The HPB canonical values are used as primary source per source priority rules.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is the well-established value for white rice (long-grain, cooked) from the University of Sydney GI Database and published in Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 72 × (25.19 × 1.80) / 100 = 72 × 45.34 / 100 ≈ 32.6, rounded to 32.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Elevated sodium (231 mg/100 g) relative to plain steamed rice; partially offset by potassium (327 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Limit portion to one bowl (180 g) and avoid adding high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or sambal; the sodium-to-potassium ratio is borderline and cumulative dietary sodium should be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) drives rapid postprandial glucose elevation; carbohydrate load per bowl is substantial (~45 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Restrict portion to 100–150 g cooked; pair with protein (e.g., tofu, fish) and non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response, and consider substituting with brown rice or basmati rice for lower GI.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate with high GI may contribute to dyslipidaemia and insulin resistance with frequent large-portion consumption; fat content (4.21 g/100 g) is atypically elevated for plain rice
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Consume in moderate portions as part of a balanced meal; the elevated fat content relative to plain rice warrants verification of the preparation method, and saturated fat data should be confirmed before clinical dietary counselling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed white rice (白饭, Nasi Putih) is a foundational staple of Singaporean cuisine, served as an accompaniment to virtually every hawker-centre and coffee-shop meal. It is prepared by washing polished long-grain white rice and steaming or boiling it until the grains are soft, fluffy, and slightly sticky. The texture is tender with a mild, neutral flavour that complements strongly seasoned dishes such as chicken curry, sambal, and braised meats. In the Singaporean context, 'Add White Rice' (加白饭) is a common menu option allowing diners to add a bowl of plain rice to any order. Per 100 g as reported by HPB Singapore, this record provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate. Note that these values are atypically high compared to plain steamed white rice in global databases (USDA: ~130 kcal, 2.7 g protein, 0.3 g fat, 28 g carbohydrate per 100 g), suggesting the HPB record may reflect a composite or enriched preparation. The glycemic index of white rice is approximately 72 (high GI), making portion control critical for individuals with type 2 diabetes. Sodium at 231 mg/100 g is elevated relative to plain rice and warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. Potassium (327 mg/100 g), calcium (54 mg/100 g), iron (2.55 mg/100 g), and magnesium (40 mg/100 g) are present at levels consistent with an enriched or composite rice dish.

confidence_reason: Input record is sourced from HPB Singapore with provenance 'canonical' and confidence 0.95, which is the highest-priority approved source for Singaporean dishes. Values are accepted as reported from the canonical HPB record.
notes: 1. NUTRIENT ANOMALY: The per-100g values in this HPB canonical record (protein 6.5 g, fat 4.21 g, dietary fibre 3.7 g, sodium 231 mg) are substantially higher than typical plain steamed white rice (USDA SR Legacy FDC 168878: protein 2.69 g, fat 0.28 g, fibre 0.4 g, sodium 1 mg per 100 g). This suggests the HPB record may represent a composite dish, enriched rice, or a preparation with added ingredients. The HPB canonical values are preserved as-is per source priority rules. A data verification request to HPB is recommended. 2. FIELDS SET TO -1 (data not available in HPB source record): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed fatty acid, vitamin, and moisture data not reported in the input HPB record and not available from the HPB Foodo database entry for this specific cr_id. 3. CHOLESTEROL: cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source record; plain rice contains no cholesterol (naturally zero). 4. GI: GI of 72 applied from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. (2008) for white rice; this is an analogous estimate as the specific HPB composite record was not individually GI-tested. 5. CULTURAL TAG: Input lists primary_cuisine as 'Malay'; reclassified to 'Singaporean' as white rice is a pan-ethnic Singaporean staple served across Chinese, Malay, Indian, and other hawker contexts. Malay ethnic tag retained in aliases. 6. SNOMED CT: snomed_ct_match_type set to 'closest_parent' as code 226355009 (Cooked rice) is the nearest available parent concept; no white-rice-specific SNOMED CT code identified.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Identified that food_name needed to be canonical ('Steamed White Rice') rather than menu-style ('Add White Rice 加白饭').
Iteration 1: Added all mandatory Singaporean multilingual local names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Iteration 1: Populated GI from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. (2008) — GI 72, high category.
Iteration 1: Computed glycemic_load_per_serving as 32.7 based on 180 g serving.
Iteration 1: Added health_context for all three required conditions with clinical guidance.
Iteration 1: Documented all -1 fields in notes per g3 requirement.
Iteration 1: Documented nutrient anomaly (HPB values atypically high vs USDA plain rice).
Iteration 1: Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases array (q8 self-check).
Iteration 2: Corrected snomed_ct_match_type from 'none' to 'broad' to align with description and gap note.

Iteration 3: Corrected snomed_ct_match_type from 'broad' to 'closest_parent' — 'broad' is not a valid enum value per the scoring model which requires one of ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. Updated snomed_ct_gap_note to reflect 'closest_parent' terminology. This resolves the G5 hard gate failure from iteration 2.
